article.title: Roche's Amylin-Based Obesity Drug Petrelintide Posts 10.7 Percent Weight Loss in Phase II but Trails GLP-1 Rivals
article.summary: Petrelintide achieves placebo-like tolerability with no vomiting at the top dose, but its 9% placebo-adjusted weight loss falls well short of Eli Lilly's competing amylin analog.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Roche and Zealand Pharma announced on March 5 that their jointly developed obesity drug petrelintide met its primary endpoint in a Phase II trial, achieving up to 10.7 percent mean weight loss from baseline at 42 weeks. The drug targets the amylin receptor rather than the GLP-1 pathway that dominates the current obesity market, and demonstrated an unusually clean tolerability profile. However, the magnitude of weight loss fell significantly short of competing therapies, raising questions about whether petrelintide can carve out a meaningful position in an increasingly crowded field.

## What We Know

The ZUPREME-1 trial enrolled 493 people with overweight or obesity at a mean baseline BMI of 37 kg/m-squared, with 53 percent of participants being female, according to [Roche](https://www.roche.com/media/releases/med-cor-2026-03-05). Participants received once-weekly subcutaneous injections across five dose-escalation arms or placebo, with doses titrated every four weeks.

All five treatment arms achieved statistically significant weight loss compared to placebo at week 28, the primary endpoint. By week 42, the highest-performing arm delivered 10.7 percent mean weight reduction versus 1.7 percent for placebo, yielding approximately 9 percent placebo-adjusted weight loss.

The tolerability data stood out as the trial's most distinctive result. According to [Roche](https://www.roche.com/media/releases/med-cor-2026-03-05), the maximally effective dose arm recorded zero cases of vomiting. Discontinuation rates due to adverse events were 4.8 percent on petrelintide versus 4.9 percent on placebo, effectively indistinguishable. Roughly 70 percent of participants at the most effective dose experienced no gastrointestinal side effects at all, as reported by [MedCity News](https://medcitynews.com/2026/03/roche-zealand-pharma-obesity-drug-petrelintide-amylin-weight-loss-zeal-rhhby/). In addition, 98 percent of participants on the highest dose arm successfully reached their maintenance dose.

Petrelintide works through the amylin pathway, a mechanism distinct from the GLP-1 receptor agonists produced by Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly that currently dominate the obesity drug market. Amylin is naturally co-secreted with insulin from pancreatic beta cells and reduces body weight by restoring sensitivity to the satiety hormone leptin, according to [Zealand Pharma](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/03/05/3250569/0/en/Zealand-Pharma-announces-positive-Phase-2-results-for-petrelintide-an-amylin-analog-with-potential-to-redefine-the-weight-management-experience-for-people-living-with-overweight-an.html).

Levi Garraway, Roche's Chief Medical Officer, stated that petrelintide "achieved meaningful weight loss with a well-tolerated dosing approach, which is essential to support long-term and sustained benefits," according to [Roche](https://www.roche.com/media/releases/med-cor-2026-03-05).

## Competitive Landscape

Despite the positive headline results, several analysts expressed skepticism about petrelintide's commercial prospects. William Blair analyst Andy Hsieh noted that the weight loss "falls short of expectations," with the firm having anticipated roughly 16 percent weight loss based on Eli Lilly's competing amylin analog eloralintide, which achieved approximately 16 percent placebo-adjusted weight loss in its own Phase II data, according to [BioSpace](https://www.biospace.com/drug-development/roche-zealands-amylin-weight-loss-drug-fails-to-clear-lillys-bar).

William Blair further stated that "the difference in weight loss of 9.0% for petrelintide versus 16% for eloralintide is too large to attribute to the enrolled population alone," as reported by [BioSpace](https://www.biospace.com/drug-development/roche-zealands-amylin-weight-loss-drug-fails-to-clear-lillys-bar). Analysts also identified what appears to be a dose-ceiling effect, where higher doses did not produce proportionally greater weight loss, suggesting limited room to improve efficacy through dose escalation alone.

For additional context, Roche's own dual GLP-1/GIP agonist CT-388 achieved 22.5 percent placebo-adjusted weight loss at 48 weeks in separate trials, according to [BioSpace](https://www.biospace.com/drug-development/roche-zealands-amylin-weight-loss-drug-fails-to-clear-lillys-bar).

## What We Don't Know

The trial reported that female participants lost "considerably more" weight than male participants, but Roche did not disclose the gender-stratified weight loss figures. It remains unclear how much of the topline result is driven by the female-majority enrollment.

The long-term durability of weight loss beyond 42 weeks is unknown. Whether amylin-mediated weight loss follows the same rebound pattern observed with GLP-1 drugs after discontinuation has not been tested.

The ZUPREME-2 trial, evaluating petrelintide in patients with obesity or overweight combined with type 2 diabetes, is expected to report topline results in the second half of 2026. A Phase II combination trial pairing petrelintide with CT-388, Roche's dual GLP-1/GIP agonist, will begin later in 2026, a strategy that could substantially alter the drug's competitive positioning if the two mechanisms prove synergistic.

## Analysis

Petrelintide's Phase II results crystallize a tension at the heart of the next-generation obesity drug race: tolerability versus raw efficacy. The drug's gastrointestinal profile is remarkably clean by the standards of a field where nausea, vomiting, and treatment discontinuation have been persistent problems. Yet in a market where Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk have trained physicians and patients to expect 15 to 25 percent weight loss, a 9 percent placebo-adjusted result positions petrelintide as a potential maintenance or adjunctive therapy rather than a frontline competitor.

Roche appears to recognize this, with its combination trial strategy signaling that petrelintide's future likely lies not as a standalone treatment but as a tolerability-enhancing partner to more aggressive weight loss agents. If the amylin pathway can meaningfully reduce the gastrointestinal side effects that drive discontinuation of GLP-1 drugs while adding incremental weight loss in combination, that would represent a genuinely useful clinical niche, even if it falls short of the blockbuster monotherapy narrative.
